title: AI-powered BI with Snowflake and Amazon Quick
summary: In this post, you will learn how to build an end-to-end integration between Snowflake semantic views and Amazon Quick. The sample data is user review data for a media company. You start by loading movie review data from Amazon Simple Storage Service (Amazon S3) into Snowflake, define a semantic view in SQL to add business meaning, explore it with natural-language queries through Cortex Analyst, and then generate an Amazon Quick dataset and dashboard. The dataset can be created manually or with a provided automation script. By the end, your BI team or AI team can ask natural-language questions against a governed data layer and trust that every response reflects the same business logic.

processed.summary:
* Implements an end-to-end integration between **Snowflake semantic views** and **Amazon QuickSight** to enable AI-driven Business Intelligence.
* Utilizes **Cortex Analyst** to provide a natural-language interface over governed data layers, ensuring consistent business logic across queries.
* Demonstrates a technical pipeline migrating **Amazon S3** data into Snowflake with **automation scripts** for dataset and dashboard generation.
* Establishes a **governed data layer** that allows both BI and AI teams to trust natural-language query responses.
key_results: Seamless integration of Snowflake semantic layers with Amazon BI ecosystems.
Enabled Natural Language Query (NLQ) capabilities via Cortex Analyst.
Reduction in business logic drift through a centralized semantic view.
Automated deployment of QuickSight datasets using provided scripts.
Proven workflow for unstructured movie review data analysis.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Snowflake integrates Cortex Analyst with Amazon QuickSight to enable governed natural-language querying for enterprise business intelligence.

title: Show HN: Nub – A Bun-like all-in-one toolkit for Node.js
summary:
Colin here, creator of Nub. I’ve had the general shape of this in mind for years. Nub runs your code with stock `node`, augmented with a `--require` preload hook[0] that adds a transpiler (oxc-powered, packaged as a Node-API add-on), registers a module resolution hook[1], and injects polyfills as needed for APIs like `Worker`, `Temporal`, etc. All purely additive, your code ultimately runs using Node’s actual engine & stdlib implementations.

[0] https://nodejs.org/api/cli.html#-require-module

[1] https://nodejs.org/api/module.html#moduleregisterhooksoption...

Comments URL: https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=48660267

Points: 201

# Comments: 57

processed.summary:
* **Nub** provides a Bun-like developer experience by augmenting standard **Node.js** with a high-performance, all-in-one toolkit architecture.
* The system leverages a **preload hook (--require)** and a **Node-API add-on** to inject an **oxc-powered transpiler** for near-instant execution of modern syntax.
* Architecture includes **custom module resolution hooks** and automated **API polyfills** for Temporal and Workers, ensuring compatibility without abandoning the native Node engine.
key_results: Native oxc-based transpilation integrated via Node-API for speed.
Automatic polyfilling for modern APIs like Temporal and Worker.
Zero-config support for TypeScript and ESM using stock Node.
Custom module resolution via standard Node.js register hooks.
Additive architecture that preserves Node's engine and stdlib.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Colin introduces Nub, a toolkit that retrofits Node.js with Bun-like features using oxc-powered transpilation and native hooks.

title: Automated sign detection across the Electronic Babylonian Library: A large-scale dataset and end-to-end cuneiform OCR pipeline
summary: Learning to read cuneiform tablets is an extremely demanding task; consequently, of the roughly half million excavated tablets, only a small fraction has been analysed by Assyriologists. Computer vision offers a promising avenue for decipherment but requires large, densely annotated datasets. To address this limitation, the largest annotated cuneiform sign dataset to date is used, and a Deformable Detection Transformer (DETR)-based object detection model is evaluated under two class granularities of 173 and 106 classes. The proposed system integrates automatic tablet-side extraction, heuristic line grouping, and n-gram-based textual similarity evaluation to bridge visual sign detection and textual structure, and achieves consistent improvements of up to 28-37% over prior work on COCO-style detection metrics. At inference, the method is applied to 87,668 tablet fragments from the Electronic Babylonian Library (eBL) corpus, producing nearly 2.9 million sign detections. Although the approach operates without linguistic priors and remains sensitive to tablet damage and layout variability, it provides a scalable and interpretable foundation for corpus-wide cuneiform analysis and supports future integration with multimodal and linguistic modelling frameworks.

processed.summary:
* Employs a **Deformable Detection Transformer (DETR)** architecture to conduct large-scale, automated sign detection on ancient cuneiform tablets.
* Integrates an end-to-end pipeline using **heuristic line grouping** and **n-gram-based similarity** to bridge the gap between visual object detection and textual structure.
* Utilizes the largest annotated dataset to date to improve sign detection accuracy by **28-37%** over existing state-of-the-art COCO-style benchmarks.
* Scales the system to process nearly **2.9 million sign detections** across the eBL corpus, providing a foundation for future **multimodal and linguistic modelling**.
key_results: Achieved 28-37% improvement over prior work on COCO-style detection metrics.
Processed 87,668 tablet fragments from the Electronic Babylonian Library corpus.
Successfully generated 2.9 million individual sign detections.
Validated model performance across two distinct class granularities (173 and 106 classes).
Developed a scalable, non-linguistic OCR pipeline resistant to tablet layout variability.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Electronic Babylonian Library researchers deploy a DETR-based pipeline to detect 2.9 million cuneiform signs, significantly outperforming prior OCR benchmarks.

title: Upstream reciprocity versus downstream reciprocity: Catalyzing cooperation
summary: Why would anyone help a stranger, knowing they may never meet again? Indirect reciprocity offers one of the most compelling evolutionary answers, yet its two canonical forms -- upstream reciprocity (experience-based), and downstream reciprocity (reputation-based) -- have been studied mostly in isolation. Their joint dynamics in finite and structured populations remain largely unexplored. Here, we fill this gap using agent-based simulations in which an agent is behaviourally either defector, upstream reciprocator, or downstream reciprocator, and the agents' population state is temporally updated using different evolutionary update mechanisms. We show that update mechanism plays a surprisingly decisive role in shaping the fate of downstream and especially upstream reciprocators. Whether agents' experiences and reputations are updated globally or locally can shift outcomes from rich behavioural coexistence to the dominance of downstream reciprocators alone. Intriguingly, we uncover a robust structural feature that persists across all the explored update rules and population sizes: an optimal network degree at which upstream reciprocity is maximized, reflecting a fundamental tug-of-war between cooperative clustering and exposure to defectors. Our results highlight that while downstream reciprocity can either foster or inhibit upstream reciprocity depending on the update mechanism, its net effect on cooperation remains largely positive.

processed.summary:
- Utilizes **agent-based simulations** to analyze the joint dynamics of upstream (experience-based) and downstream (reputation-based) reciprocity within finite, structured populations.
- Examines how **evolutionary update mechanisms**, specifically local versus global updates, shift population states from rich behavioral coexistence to the total dominance of downstream reciprocators.
- Investigates the **structural topology** of networks to identify how connection density impacts the balance between cooperative clustering and vulnerability to defectors.
key_results: Evolutionary update mechanisms are the primary factor in determining whether upstream reciprocators survive.
Downstream reciprocity generally acts as a catalyst, rather than a competitor, for overall population cooperation.
An optimal network degree exists across all models where upstream reciprocity reaches its maximum effectiveness.
Local update rules tend to favor reputation-based agents over experience-based agents.
The presence of downstream reciprocators can either foster or inhibit upstream reciprocity depending on the specific update rule used.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that update mechanisms and network structures dictate the success of experience-based versus reputation-based cooperative agents.

title: Stationary Robust Mean-Field Games under Model Mismatches
summary: Deploying multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) in the real world is often limited by model mismatches between the training simulators and the true environment, which could be further amplified through strategic interactions and result in severe performance degradation upon deployment. Distributional robustness offers a principled response by optimizing policies against worst-case transition models drawn from an uncertainty set, but standard robust MARL frameworks become increasingly intractable as the number of agents grows. This paper develops an infinite-horizon, stationary mean-field game framework that incorporates distributional model uncertainty directly into the population-coupled dynamics. We establish a robust dynamic programming principle with a contractive Bellman operator and prove the existence of a stationary robust mean-field equilibrium via a fixed-point argument. We further develop the first concrete algorithm with convergence guarantees. We then connect the mean-field solution to a finite-population robust game whose ambiguity sets depend on the empirical distribution, showing that the mean-field equilibrium policy induces approximate equilibrium behavior as the population size increases. Under a contractive robust-dynamics regime, we further obtain explicit non-asymptotic error bounds. Numerical experiments further illustrate the qualitative and quantitative impact of robustness under multiple uncertainty models, validating our theoretical findings.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a framework for **Stationary Mean-Field Games** (MFG) that integrates distributional robustness to solve model mismatch issues in multi-agent reinforcement learning.
- Establishes a **Robust Dynamic Programming** principle by utilizing a contractive Bellman operator, proving the existence of a stationary robust mean-field equilibrium.
- Bridges the gap between infinite-population models and **Finite-Population Robust Games**, demonstrating that mean-field policies remain effective as population size increases.
- Introduces the first algorithmic approach for robust MFGs with **provable convergence guarantees** and non-asymptotic error bounds.
key_results: Formal proof of stationary robust mean-field equilibrium existence via fixed-point arguments.
Derivation of a robust Bellman operator that maintains contraction properties under model uncertainty.
Development of a concrete algorithm for robust MFGs with explicit convergence guarantees.
Establishment of non-asymptotic error bounds connecting mean-field solutions to finite-agent systems.
Validation of the framework's resilience against multiple uncertainty models through numerical experiments.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a robust mean-field game framework to stabilize large-scale multi-agent systems against environment model mismatches.

title: What Characterizes Pairwise Modular Smells?
summary:
Enhancing the modular structure of existing systems has attracted substantial research interest, primarily through (1) software modularization and (2) identifying design issues (e.g., smells) as refactoring opportunities; however, both approaches often prove impractical to guide effective improvement. Inspired by both aforementioned approaches, our previous study introduced a novel and practical architectural smell -- called Pairwise Modular Smell (or PairSmell) -- for identifying flawed architectural decisions that necessitate further examination.
  The objective of this study is to explain PairSmell from the perspective of pair characteristics. To this end, we first conduct a rapid review to collect and synthesize 19 pair characteristics that have been used in the literature to represent relationships between two entities. The collected characteristics are then used to train machine learning models for predicting two forms of PairSmell -- inapt separated pairs InSep and inapt collocated pairs InCol, based on a curated dataset of over 6,135,000 pairs of entities derived from 11 open-source Java projects. The trained models achieve up to a 58.6% improvement in ROC-AUC over the baselines. The interpretation of the models reveals that the most influential features for InSep are out-going dependencies, terms shared with others, and declared fields; while those for InCol include semantic similarity based on tf-idf, terms shared between the pair, terms shared with others, and in-going dependencies. We complement the work with a series of practical examples to illustrate how the influential pair characteristics impact the occurrence of PairSmell.

processed.summary:
- Introduced the concept of **Pairwise Modular Smell (PairSmell)** to identify flawed architectural decisions using two categories: inaptly separated pairs (**InSep**) and inaptly collocated pairs (**InCol**).
- Developed a **Machine Learning framework** trained on 19 distinct pair characteristics synthesized from a literature review to automate the detection of these modularity issues.
- Analyzed a massive dataset of over **6.1 million entity pairs** across 11 Java projects to validate the predictive power of structural and semantic features.
- Discovered that **semantic similarity** and **dependency patterns** (in-going and out-going) are the most influential factors in determining architectural health.
key_results: Identified 19 unique pair characteristics for representing relationships between software entities.
Achieved up to a 58.6% improvement in ROC-AUC compared to existing baseline models.
Found that out-going dependencies and shared terms are the strongest predictors for InSep smells.
Established that semantic similarity based on tf-idf is the primary driver for identifying InCol smells.
Validated findings using a large-scale dataset derived from 11 major open-source Java projects.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers utilize machine learning to analyze pairwise characteristics, significantly improving the detection of architectural smells in software systems.

title: Event-by-event fluctuations of elliptic flow in ultrarelativistic O+O collisions
summary: We study O+O collisions at $\sqrt{s_\mathrm{NN}} = 5.36$ TeV within a fully three-dimensional $\text{McDipper}$+$\text{MUSIC}$ model, which allows us to describe the experimentally measured dependence of charged hadron multiplicity on centrality and pseudorapidity. We show that the initial elliptical eccentricity is mainly driven by the fluctuations of the energy deposition and thereby varies considerably event-by-event within a fixed centrality class. This also holds for elliptic flow $v_2$, whose origin in O+O thus differs from that in collisions of heavy nuclei. Using a decomposition of initial states in an average event and uncorrelated modes, we find that despite the large size of fluctuations we can reproduce the joint probability distribution of eccentricity and elliptic flow with a reasonable accuracy with only a small set of fluctuation modes.

processed.summary:
- Utilizes a fully **three-dimensional McDipper+MUSIC** hydrodynamic model to simulate ultrarelativistic O+O collisions at 5.36 TeV.
- Analyzes the **event-by-event fluctuations** of energy deposition to determine the origins of initial elliptical eccentricity.
- Establishes that **fluctuation modes** dominate the elliptic flow characteristics in smaller systems compared to traditional heavy nuclei collisions.
key_results: Successfully simulated O+O collisions matching experimental charged hadron multiplicity and pseudorapidity.
Identified that initial elliptical eccentricity is primarily driven by energy deposition fluctuations.
Determined that O+O elliptic flow mechanisms differ fundamentally from those in heavy nuclei.
Decomposed initial states into an average event plus uncorrelated fluctuation modes.
Reproduced joint probability distributions of eccentricity and flow using a limited set of modes.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that O+O collision elliptic flow is driven by energy fluctuations rather than average nuclear geometry.

title: Physics-Informed Predictive Control for Integrated Electric-Vehicle Thermal Management: An Open, Real-Data-Anchored Benchmark
summary: Thermal management in a battery-electric vehicle (BEV) is a coupled, vehicle-level problem: the battery pack, the passenger cabin, the heat pump, and cabin air quality compete for shared actuation and energy, yet most studies optimise a single subsystem on proprietary models, which prevents fair, reproducible comparison. We present OpenEV-ThermoSciML, an open and reproducible benchmark that couples a battery electro-thermal-aging model, a two-node cabin model, a heat-pump/HVAC model, and a CO$_2$/ventilation model under real driving cycles (EPA) and real weather (NREL TMY3, NASA POWER), scored by a multi-objective suite spanning battery health, PMV/PPD comfort, cabin air quality, and HVAC energy. The benchmark's battery thermal core is anchored and validated on real BEV battery-management-system (BMS) data; the reduced battery (two-state) and cabin (two-node) models are validated against converged higher-fidelity references and, for the cabin, independently cross-checked against EnergyPlus 25.2.0. On top of the benchmark we develop a physics-informed scientific-machine-learning (Sci-ML) surrogate -- a nominal-physics prior plus a learned residual with conservation penalties -- that is exact on conserved quantities and dominates black-box and Koopman surrogates out-of-distribution (overall rollout RMSE 0.014 vs 1.168 and 3.991). A shielded Sci-ML model-predictive controller (MPC) delivers statistically significant, all-positive improvements over a production-like rule-based controller across six scenarios -- including a real hot-day US06 trip (energy $-15\%$, comfort RMSE $-47\%$, peak CO$_2$ $-25\%$, battery thermal-gradient $-78\%$) -- and these gains transfer to an independently exported OpenModelica 8-node co-simulation plant.

processed.summary:
- Introduction of **OpenEV-ThermoSciML**, an open-source benchmark for integrated electric vehicle thermal management that couples battery, cabin, HVAC, and air quality systems.
- Development of a **Scientific Machine Learning (Sci-ML) surrogate** model that incorporates a nominal-physics prior and learned residuals with conservation penalties for physical consistency.
- Implementation of a **shielded Sci-ML Model Predictive Controller (MPC)** designed to optimize multi-objective trade-offs including energy efficiency and battery health.
- The framework is anchored in **real-world data** from BMS systems, EPA driving cycles, and NASA/NREL weather data, ensuring high-fidelity validation.
key_results: Sci-ML surrogate achieved a rollout RMSE of 0.014, significantly outperforming black-box models (1.168) and Koopman surrogates (3.991).
The MPC delivered a 15% reduction in HVAC energy consumption during real-world US06 driving scenarios.
Passenger comfort metrics (PMV/PPD) saw a 47% improvement compared to standard rule-based controllers.
Battery thermal gradients were reduced by 78%, potentially extending battery cycle life and safety.
Peak CO2 levels in the cabin were reduced by 25% through optimized ventilation management.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: OpenEV-ThermoSciML researchers introduce a physics-informed Sci-ML framework that significantly improves EV thermal management and energy efficiency via MPC.

title: Submodular Welfare Maximization with Budget Constraints in the Random-Order Model
summary: We study an online item-allocation problem with budgets and a submodular objective. A set of $m$ agents is known in advance, and each agent $j$ has a known budget. A set of $n$ items arrives over time in a uniformly random order. When item $i$ arrives, its cost $c_{i,j}$ for each agent $j$ is revealed, and the algorithm must irrevocably assign $i$ to an agent without violating any budget constraint. The goal is to maximize a monotone submodular function defined over all possible assignments $[n] \times [m]$. At the time of decision, the algorithm has only oracle access to this submodular function restricted to items seen so far. This model subsumes welfare maximization with submodular valuations, agent-specific item costs, and agent-specific budgets. We measure the performance of an algorithm by its competitive ratio, i.e., the worst-case ratio between the algorithm's expected value and that of the offline optimum, which knows all item costs and the full submodular function in advance. Prior work only considered the case of a single agent and achieved a $1/54.4$-competitive algorithm. We generalize and improve this result to a polynomial-time $α$-competitive algorithm with $α\approx 1/14.85$ for an arbitrary number of agents. We also study the special case in which all item costs and all budgets equal $1$, which yields an online submodular matching problem. Prior work achieved a polynomial $1/9.66$-competitive algorithm for this problem; we improve this to a factor of $1/6.86$. Both our algorithms rely on repeatedly computing $(1 - 1/e)$-approximations of the multilinear extensions of offline variants of the subproblems. If super-polynomial runtime is allowed, these subproblems can be solved optimally, and our competitive ratios improve by this factor.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a novel polynomial-time algorithm for **online item-allocation** where items arrive in a random order and must be assigned to agents under strict **budget constraints**.
- Generalizes previous models by supporting **monotone submodular functions** across multiple agents, accommodating agent-specific costs and individual budget limits.
- Utilizes a strategy of repeatedly computing **(1 - 1/e)-approximations** of multilinear extensions for offline subproblem variants to make real-time assignment decisions.
- Establishes new theoretical benchmarks for **competitive ratios**, significantly outperforming prior single-agent and unit-cost specialized algorithms.
key_results: Improved the competitive ratio for multi-agent submodular welfare maximization from 1/54.4 to approximately 1/14.85.
Enhanced the performance bound for online submodular matching with unit costs from 1/9.66 to 1/6.86.
Validated a polynomial-time approach for an arbitrary number of agents in a random-order model.
Demonstrated that competitive ratios can be further improved if super-polynomial runtime is permitted for offline subproblems.
Unified submodular valuations, agent-specific costs, and budget constraints into a single theoretical framework.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers improve competitive ratios for online submodular welfare maximization with budgets using random-order item arrival models.

title: I make good money. Why do I still feel like this?

- The **Linux Kernel** has finalized a six-year engineering effort to eliminate the deprecated **strncpy** API from the core codebase.
- The architectural transition involved **360 patches** across various subsystems to replace unsafe string handling with the more secure **strscpy** function.
- This change addresses long-standing **buffer overflow** and information leak risks by ensuring consistent NUL-termination and preventing unnecessary memory padding.
key_results: Full removal of the strncpy API after a six-year refactoring process.
Implementation of 360 distinct patches to update string handling logic.
Standardization on strscpy for guaranteed NUL-termination in kernel space.
Reduction of potential security vulnerabilities related to memory corruption.
Elimination of performance overhead caused by strncpy's excessive zero-padding.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Linux Kernel developers successfully eliminate the strncpy API to improve security and prevent memory-related vulnerabilities across the codebase.

- **Loupe** is an open-source iOS utility designed to monitor and expose the background data collection activities of native mobile applications.
- The tool focuses on tracking access to the **system pasteboard**, **local network scanning**, and detailed **photo library metadata** that apps often exploit without explicit user awareness.
- It provides a technical diagnostic interface for researchers to observe how third-party SDKs implement **device fingerprinting** and silent data exfiltration techniques.
- The project serves as a transparency layer to audit the effectiveness of Apple's **Privacy manifest** and sandbox restrictions in real-world scenarios.
key_results: Identifies apps that perform unauthorized local network discovery to map user environments.
Logs frequency and timing of pasteboard access to detect clipboard hijacking.
Visualizes the granular EXIF and location data accessible through photo library permissions.
Provides a real-time event feed of system API calls related to privacy-sensitive information.
Demonstrates common bypasses used by trackers to identify unique device hardware profiles.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Mysk Research releases Loupe to visualize how native iOS apps exploit system permissions and metadata for device fingerprinting and data harvesting.
